mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of an adjacency matrix representation for a graph with V vertices?
mcqs.explanation: An adjacency matrix is a V x V 2D array. Its size, and thus its space complexity, is always O(V²), regardless of the number of edges (E).

mcqs.question: For an undirected graph with no self-loops, what property does its adjacency matrix always have?
mcqs.explanation: In an undirected graph, an edge from u to v implies an edge from v to u. This means adjMatrix[u][v] = adjMatrix[v][u], making the matrix symmetric across its main diagonal.

mcqs.question: Which graph representation is generally more efficient for performing a Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversal on a sparse graph?
mcqs.explanation: BFS involves visiting all neighbors of a node. An adjacency list allows this in O(degree(u)) time per node, which is optimal. An adjacency matrix requires O(V) time per node to scan the entire row, which is inefficient for sparse graphs where degree(u) is much less than V.

mcqs.question: Consider a directed graph represented by an adjacency list. What is the time complexity to check for the existence of a specific edge (u, v)?
mcqs.explanation: To check for an edge (u, v) in an adjacency list, you must search through the list of neighbors for vertex u. The length of this list is the out-degree of u. In the worst case, this is O(V), but the precise complexity is O(degree(u)).

mcqs.question: A complete graph with 10 vertices has 45 edges. If you need to frequently check for the existence of any arbitrary edge, which representation is most time-efficient?

options.text: Both Matrix and List are equally efficient.

mcqs.explanation: A complete graph is dense. While both representations are feasible, an adjacency matrix provides constant-time O(1) edge lookups (a direct array access), which is faster than the O(degree(u)) lookup of an adjacency list. Since the graph is complete, degree(u) is V-1, which is O(V).
